title: Dungeon Universalis - Take a Look - Part Two
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=XEqxbZoaqUw

Part two of Daniel (@dungeondive)'s deep dive opens by clarifying a comment from part one: the dark-player role in **Dungeon Universalis** is not adversarial in the Descent 2.0 sense. The dark player has no independent victory condition — only the heroes have goals — and the dark player's strength scales automatically with the heroes' value points rather than from winning scenarios. Daniel argues this means the right way to play is as a non-adversarial DM facilitating fun, not racing the heroes.

From there it's a tour of the cardboard. Cardboard player mats accept either pre-made character cards or custom-creation tear-off sheets. The chit count is mercifully restrained — a single jewellery box holds them all — though Daniel notes the chits are single-sided, an oversight Oscar has acknowledged. The big cardboard payoff is the *tiles*: a vast variety of dungeon rooms, outdoor environments (cobblestone docks, lava, deserts, rivers, indoor taverns, magical pools, jail cells, throne rooms, spiral staircases). Plus dungeon-feature overlays — alchemist's tables, blacksmithing stations, fireplaces, pillars, trap doors, treasure chests — that interact with rules, not just decoration.

The standee count is enormous — mediums numbered 1 to 147, plus large and extra-large categories — with stats printed on the back. Daniel laments the lack of custom-shaped cuts but acknowledges the cost trade-off was probably right.

----

Daniel argues that *non-adversarial dungeon-master design* — where the dark player's strength scales with the heroes rather than against them — is the right model for boxed dungeon crawls. Have you seen this model work in other games, or is it inherently a board-game-meets-RPG hybrid?
